dataBlog.title: Weather App (React Native)
dataBlog.subtitle: — My First Mobile Project

overview.description: This is my first React Native project — a mobile weather application with real-time data, hourly forecasts, and a multi-day forecast screen. My goal was not just to build a visually appealing UI, but to go through the full development cycle: API integration, state management, handling incomplete data, and building stable application logic.

learningGoals: Build real mobile UI with React Native
Design component-based architecture with clear responsibilities
Debug issues across API and UI layers

challenge.title: Most Interesting Challenge
challenge.description: During testing, I noticed some forecast days were rendered as instead of real values.At first, it looked like an API issue, but the root cause was mixed:
problem: The UI always expected 7 forecast days
The API sometimes returned fewer days (e.g. 3 depending on plan/conditions)
The interface rendered placeholder entries for missing days

dataBlog.result: The UI now accurately reflects real API data: no fake empty days and no confusing placeholders for users.
solution: Refactored the daily list rendering to map only existing forecast entries
Made the forecast title dynamic based on actual day count
Removed forced placeholders for non-existent days
lessonsLearned: Never assume API responses are always complete in real-world conditions.
Build defensive UI for incomplete and variable data.
Avoid rigid assumptions in component logic.
Debug faster by separating data-layer and UI-layer investigation.
Treat frontend work as engineering, not only styling.
dataBlog.conclusion: For a first React Native project, this was strong practical experience: from external API integration to solving a real UX bug. This case study shows how I approach problems as a developer: identify root cause, fix architecture, improve user experience.
